license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.pex-1955/v1
title: Bacteriophage DNA Organic Extraction
author: Fabian Kunisch

steps_raw.instruction: Add 1 ml of phage stock solution (10 9 -10 10 PFU/ml) to a 2 ml microcentrifuge tube.
Add 10 µl of DNase I (1 mg/ml) and 10 µl RNase A (5 mg/ml) to the tube.
Place the tube in a shaker thermoblock and incubate for 60 min at 37 °C.
Add 40 µl of 0.5 M EDTA-Na 2 to the tube.
Add 2.5 µl of Proteinase K (20 mg/ml) to the tube.
Add 50 µl of 10 % SDS to the tube.
Place the tube in a shaker thermoblock and incubate for 1 h at 56 °C.
Let the tube cool down to room temperature (approx. 20 min).
Transfer the entire volume of the microcentrifuge tube into a 15 ml centrifuge tube.
Add 1.1 ml of phenol to the centrifuge tube, seal it with the lid and invert it 5 times.
Centrifuge the tube for 5 min at 22 °C and 4,000 g.
Transfer the upper aqueous phase into a new 15 ml centrifuge tube.
Add the same volume of phenol/chloroform in a ratio of 50/50 (e.g., for 0.9 ml of extracted volume in step 12, add 450 µl phenol and 450 µl chloroform).
Centrifuge the tube for 5 min at 22 °C and 4,000 g.
Transfer the upper aqueous phase into a new 15 ml centrifuge tube.
Add the same volume of chloroform (e.g., for 700 µl of extracted volume in step 15, add 700 µl chloroform).
Centrifuge the tube for 5 min at 22 °C and 4,000 g.
Repeat steps 16 and 17.
Transfer a maximum of 550 µl of the upper aqueous phase into a 2 ml microcentrifuge tube.
Add a maximum of 55 µl (1/10 th of volume in step 19) sodium acetate to the tube.
Add a maximum of 1375 µl (2.5x volumes after step 20) ethanol (95 %).
Incubate the tube for 30 min on ice in a freezer (-20 °C).
Centrifuge the tube for 20 min at 4 °C and 14,000 g.
Remove the supernatant and add 2 ml of ethanol (70 %) to the tube.
Invert the tube 10 times and incubate it for 5 min at room temperature.
Centrifuge the tube for 5 min at 4 °C and 14,000 g.
Remove the supernatant and let the pellet dry on air (approx. 20 min).
Resuspend the pellet in 20 µl ddH 2 O.
Store the solution in a refrigerator at 4 °C until further use.
Using a spectrophotometer (e.g., NanoDrop 1000 Spectrophotometer; Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA; cat. no.: ND-1000), the concentration of phage DNA and its purity can be evaluated.

Step: 23 & 26 Problem: No pellet visible Possible reason: Low phage starting concentration Solution: Increase the phage concentration at step 1 Step: 23 & 26 Problem: No pellet visible Possible reason: Pellet may be difficult to visualise (colourless) Solution: Continue extraction and determine DNA concentration at the end Problem: No DNA present Possible reason: RNA virus Solution: Requires a different protocol Further modifications of the protocol might include the addition of isoamyl alcohol to the phenol/chloroform (step 13; ratio: 25 ml : 24 ml : 1 ml) and the chloroform (step 16; ratio: 24 ml : 1 ml) steps, as an anti-foaming agent and to support the DNA purification. Additionally, the duration of the ethanol treatment could be increased to 6 h at -20 °C (step 22).
Approximately 4:20 h will be required to complete the DNA extraction, including 3:30 h wait time.

With this protocol, clean phage DNA can be sufficiently extracted and used in downstream analysis. Depending on the phage, the phage production method or the initial phage concentration, extracted DNA concentrations ranging from 50 to 1000 ng/µl (260/280 ratio: ∼1.8) can be expected.

Bacteriophage–antibiotic combinations against ciprofloxacin/ceftriaxone-resistant Escherichia coli in vitro and in an experimental Galleria mellonella model by Lei Wang, Tamta Tkhilaishvili, Beatriz Bernal Andres, Andrej Trampuz, Mercedes Gonzalez Moreno published in International Journal of Antimicrobial Agents on 17 Oct, 2020 https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijantimicag.2020.106200
